items.title: The relationship recession is even bigger for Gen Z than we thought
items.summary: We know that members of Gen Z are less likely to be in a steady relationship than millennials were at their age, but previous research missed out an important factor that actually widens the relationship recession

items.title: Killer robots are here – we must finally decide whether to accept them
items.summary: We can no longer ignore the growing threat of fully autonomous weapons. The world must either act to ban them or accept that they are the future of war

items.title: Quantum computer quickly mines cryptocurrency while using less energy
items.summary: A superconducting quantum computer is part of a network that is mining an experimental cryptocurrency called Quip, and it is able to do it faster and with better energy efficiency than conventional machines

items.title: How to sparkle in conversation with strangers
items.summary: In the face of loneliness, many people are turning to AI chatbots for companionship – but research shows it can’t replace human connection. Columnist David Robson explores how beneficial it can be to talk to strangers, w

items.title: First working nuclear clock heralds a new era in timekeeping
items.summary: A clock based on radioactive thorium atoms realises a long-held ambition, demonstrating a technology that could eventually beat the accuracy of today’s best atomic clocks

items.title: Global map reveals the vast scale of underground fungal networks
items.summary: Our soils are teeming with networks of fungi, and we're starting to understand how important they are

items.title: Have we finally worked out how Venus flytraps snap shut?
items.summary: It was widely thought that the movement of water through Venus flytrap cells caused the trap to close, but detailed experiments have led scientists to propose an alternative mechanism

items.title: El Niño has started and the weather could get weird
items.summary: Global weather agencies have declared that El Niño has begun, and models show it is more likely than not to be a "super" El Niño. The climate pattern boosts extreme weather around the world, and could lead to record temp

items.title: Toy universe shows that time could be a quantum illusion
items.summary: An experiment with a toy universe made up of extremely cold atoms shows how time can emerge from quantum interactions, instead of existing by default

items.title: Dramatic photo of ibis being guided to their winter homes wins award
items.summary: Student Gunnar Hartmann wins Nature’s 2026 Scientist at Work photography competition for this shot of migrating northern bald ibis in Spain

items.title: The one film to watch before seeing Steven Spielberg’s Disclosure Day
items.summary: With Steven Spielberg’s new extraterrestrial film Disclosure Day just out, it’s the ideal time to watch Close Encounter of the Third Kind – perhaps the perfect UFO film, says film columnist Bethan Ackerley

items.title: Vaping after quitting smoking is linked to lung cancer
items.summary: A study of 4.5 million people suggests that ex-smokers who take up vaping are more at risk of dying from lung cancer than people who quit without the use of e-cigarettes

items.title: Art and nature come together in stunning new Henry Moore exhibition
items.summary: A visit to Kew Gardens’ exhibit of the sculptor’s work is a fascinating insight into how he was inspired by nature

items.title: Sci-fi horror film Backrooms is a triumph for its 20-year-old director
items.summary: With its origins in a creepy image posted on 4chan, Backrooms is an unusually potent big-screen experiment in fear and perception, says Davide Abbatescianni

items.title: Striking photos show how sands are encroaching on oases in the Sahara
items.summary: A photo essay from Tommy Trenchard explores efforts to protect the fragile ecosystems of oases in Chad

items.title: Think you have a good sense of humour? So do most people…
items.summary: Feedback is alarmed by a study that explored how funny people think they are, and discovered certain traits in those who rate themselves the most humorous

items.title: New Scientist recommends a brilliant take on the evolution of birds
items.summary: Palaeontologist Steve Brusatte's The Story of Birds offers an excellent and sometimes startling account of bird evolution, finds Michael Marshall

items.title: Wolves seen hunting European bison in rare camera-trap recording
items.summary: Europe’s largest land animal, the bison, is thought to be relatively unthreatened by predators, but footage from Białowieża Primaeval Forest in Poland shows it does face attacks from wolves

items.title: Millions of fossil whale bones found in deep-ocean ‘necropolis’
items.summary: Researchers diving 7 kilometres deep in a crewed submersible have discovered a vast collection of whale bones, including fossils up to 5 million years old and species new to science

items.title: Hundreds of new moons are revealing our solar system's violent history
items.summary: The outer solar system once seemed like a quiet backwater. But a glut of tiny, strange moons with unruly orbits are coming into view, revealing hints of a surprising past – and the origin of Saturn's rings

items.title: A nuclear war between India and Pakistan could destroy the ozone layer
items.summary: Climate models suggest a small nuclear war in the tropics would do even more damage to the ozone layer than a larger nuclear war in more northerly latitudes, increasing exposure to dangerous ultraviolet radiation all ove

items.title: Fully autonomous drones have killed human soldiers for the first time
items.summary: A senior figure in the Ukrainian defence industry told New Scientist that a test took place two years ago involving fully autonomous drones set to destroy anything in a given area, with confirmed casualties

items.title: A Waymo nearly hit me, but I'm still optimistic about driverless cars
items.summary: A near miss with a Waymo while cycling through London hasn't changed my optimistic stance on driverless cars, but we can't ever let our guard down, says Matthew Sparkes

items.title: Robots are about to overtake armed soldiers as the deciders of war
items.summary: Uncrewed ground vehicles have already been tested for defending the front line by the Ukrainian military. Despite their limitations, these remotely controlled robots could be the deciding factor in many conflicts

items.title: Iron Age Britons may have removed the brains of the dead
items.summary: Scrape marks inside a skull and sharpened limb bones in a set of remains found in Scotland may be evidence of unusual Iron Age funerary rituals

items.title: Frozen squirrel scat preserves ancient DNA from hundreds of species
items.summary: A complex ecosystem of woolly mammoths, bison, horses and big cats has been elucidated by studying the faeces of small rodents that probably ate the bigger animals

items.title: The last-ditch plan to save coral reefs from utter destruction
items.summary: Bleaching has devastated reefs around the world, raising fears of an irreversible shift. Yet new interventions have revealed that corals can be remarkably resilient if we can give them enough help to recover

items.title: Dinosaur-killing asteroid impact site stayed hot for millions of years
items.summary: Drill cores at the impact site of the Chicxulub asteroid show evidence that, alongside widespread destruction, the collision created a vast underground ecosystem filled with hot water that sheltered microbial life

items.title: A cosmic case of mistaken identity that can only be solved right now
items.summary: Brown dwarfs are somewhere between the size of a planet and a star, so how could we have potentially mistaken two of them for distant galaxies? Columnist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein argues that solving this cosmic mix-up is

items.title: Why we should all take quantum physics extremely personally
items.summary: Physics is considered a cold, hard science – but it will transform your life if you view it with a bit more subjectivity, says Karmela Padavic-Callaghan

items.title: You don't need to worry about recursive-self-improving AI – yet
items.summary: Anthropic has warned that recursive-self-improving AI could be on the horizon, but the truth is the company is more immediately concerned with marketing itself for a blockbuster initial public offering on the stock marke

items.title: What really happened when ancient humans migrated out of Africa
items.summary: The out-of-Africa migration, in which ancient humans went on to inhabit every other continent except Antarctica, may not have been one moment in time, but a long and slow process. Columnist Michael Marshall examines how

items.title: What is a ‘normal’ memory slowdown, and when should I worry?
items.summary: Lapses in memory are a normal part of ageing but can also be signs of dementia. Here’s how to distinguish between typical brain ageing and cognitive decline

items.title: Wildlife thrives in solar farm built on restored peatland
items.summary: A diverse range of bird species has been recorded at a solar park on rewetted peatland in Germany, suggesting that combining energy generation with habitat restoration could benefit biodiversity, the climate and the econ

items.title: Can Apple and Google stop children from sharing explicit images?
items.summary: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer has warned tech firms, including Apple and Google, that they must voluntarily implement tools to stop children sharing explicit images, but experts warn this is easier said than done

items.title: Half the world's reservoirs could be clogged up with dirt by 2060
items.summary: Each decade the world is losing over 7 per cent of its freshwater storage capacity to sediment build-up, according to an analysis of over half a million reservoirs

items.title: Understanding anorexia’s grip on the brain could unlock new therapies
items.summary: One-third of people with anorexia nervosa don’t recover and treatment has remained stagnant for years. Now we’re beginning to understand how the condition takes over the mind

items.title: Unpicking endometriosis reveals how it affects more than the pelvis
items.summary: Endometriosis is usually thought of as a gynaecological condition, but a huge study shows it has links with cholesterol levels, inflammation and an altered microbiome

items.title: You could get some of the benefits of sleep without having to nod off
items.summary: Mice seemed to reap some of the benefits of sleep by having their brain activity stimulated while they were awake, and the researchers plan to test the approach on people

items.title: Are we getting to the point where it's safe to gene-edit babies?
items.summary: A team in the US has reported promising results after using an improved form of CRISPR to gene-edit human embryos, but a major issue remains unsolved

items.title: Cuts to US ocean programme will hinder monitoring of El Niño and AMOC
items.summary: Scientists warn that the Trump administration's push to dismantle a vital network of ocean-sensing instruments will stymie crucial weather and climate monitoring in the Pacific and Atlantic

items.title: Flood of AI 'garbage' is pushing open-source developers to the limit
items.summary: The modern world depends on open-source software maintained by volunteers, but the added demands of checking and fixing AI-written submissions are causing some to burn out and quit

items.title: A chromosome from a frozen rat has been resurrected inside mice
items.summary: Mice that contain cells with an added rat chromosome have been created by scientists. The next step is to try this with frozen elephant tissue – and if that works, the team will try it with frozen mammoths

items.title: The maths meme that has been distracting mathematicians for a century
items.summary: A seemingly simple set of rules kicks off a kind of mathematical magic trick, which has kept great minds busy since the 1930s. Columnist Jacob Aron explores the origins of the Collatz conjecture, why it is so addictive t

items.title: Becoming a parent may make you love your partner less
items.summary: Parents report loving their partners less within the first year of having a child, but that doesn't mean the feeling is permanent or inevitable

items.title: Mysterious ‘cold blob’ in the Atlantic suggests the AMOC is weakening
items.summary: A patch of ocean south-east of Greenland is the only place on Earth that is cooling, and it could be a sign that the warm water "conveyor belt" in the Atlantic is slowing down

items.title: How Rachel Carson's Silent Spring changed the world in 1962
items.summary: Rachel Carson’s look at the dire effects of industrial and agricultural pollution birthed the modern environmental movement when it was first published – and remains as crucial a read today, finds Rowan Hooper

items.title: Stonehenge's altar stone probably wasn't transported by a glacier
items.summary: A glacier could have carried the giant sandstone at the centre of Stonehenge southwards from north-east Scotland, but this scenario appears unlikely

items.title: Everyone is Lying to You for Money is a must-watch exposé of crypto
items.summary: Actor Ben McKenzie explores the world of crypto in an entertaining documentary that doesn't shy away from calling out those who have promoted the currency

items.title: The looming El Niño could be bad – but much worse is to come
items.summary: Global warming will amplify the impacts of El Niño events, and could also make them much stronger and more far-reaching

items.title: Explore the mind-bending and paradoxical art of M C. Escher
items.summary: A new retrospective of the artist beloved by mathematicians opens this week. Get up close to the art with our interactive story

items.title: Superintelligent machines may well need us after all
items.summary: Despite AI's dizzying improvements in mathematical ability, its successes show just how integral human mathematicians are to the scientific process

items.title: Alice Roberts: 'We are fundamentally, at the end of the day, animals'
items.summary: Why do we have big brains? Or walk on two legs? Biological anthropologist and broadcaster Alice Roberts talks human exceptionalism, evolution and her new book Humans with Michael Marshall

items.title: New Scientist recommends a deep dive into our organs by Giulia Enders
items.summary: Giulia Enders made her name with Gut, an exploration of our intestines. Now, in the compelling follow-up Organ Speak, she’s listening to what our other organs are telling us

items.title: Earth has a mysterious triple symmetry that may influence its climate
items.summary: A circle running along the 27° east and 153° west meridians divides the globe into two halves with equal reflectivity – and this may have implications for solar geoengineering schemes

items.title: Ditch the niceties in AI prompts to save energy use, say researchers
items.summary: A UN report warns of the rapid growth in AI energy consumption, but suggests users can improve efficiency by making prompts more concise

items.title: Atom-based quantum computers are catching up in the race to usefulness
items.summary: A quantum computer made from extremely cold atoms can correct its own errors during long computations, an important prerequisite for becoming truly useful

items.title: Keto diet shows real promise for anorexia recovery
items.summary: Restricting carbohydrates may sound like an unlikely approach to treating anorexia, but following a ketogenic diet was linked to recovery in nearly 75 per cent of people with the eating disorder in a small trial

items.title: Ötzi's frozen remains may harbour metabolically active microbes
items.summary: Researchers studying a 5300-year-old mummified man have identified bacteria that lived in his gut when he was alive, as well as cold-tolerant fungi that colonised his body after death

items.title: Why you need to future-proof your brain in middle age and how to start
items.summary: Ages 40 to 65 see a period of turmoil in the brain that has previously been overlooked. But identifying problems during this time can protect your cognitive health for decades to come

items.title: How the electromagnetic spectrum opened our eyes to the universe
items.summary: Our understanding of the electromagnetic spectrum goes back to Isaac Newton, but astronomers are still finding new ways to employ it. Astrophysicist Emma Chapman explores how much these invisible waves can reveal to us a

items.title: The best new popular science books of June 2026
items.summary: The most exciting popular science reads this month explore everything from symbiosis to hormones, while Alice Roberts takes on an editor-in-chief role in her latest book

items.title: Hearing loss is bad for the whole body – but new treatments are coming
items.summary: From dementia to heart attacks, hearing loss has been linked to a wide range of effects across the body, and the condition is on the rise. Fortunately, we're learning how best to safeguard this crucial sense and how we m

items.title: Hidden store of manganese may have helped Earth get its oxygen
items.summary: Computer simulations have uncovered a new manganese compound that could exist deep in Earth’s mantle and may be connected to the process that gave our atmosphere oxygen

items.title: New Scientist recommends Togetherness, a radical new view of life
items.summary: An exploration of how biological cooperation underpins all life - and why we’ve overlooked its power until now - makes thrilling reading, finds Penny Sarchet

items.title: 'Transformative' pancreatic cancer drug doubles survival time
items.summary: People with advanced pancreatic cancer taking an experimental daily pill lived nearly twice as long as those receiving chemotherapy infusions

items.title: Do turmeric and curcumin have any actual health benefits?
items.summary: Turmeric is heralded for its anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory properties, but columnist Alice Klein finds that the evidence for this is shaky. Taking high doses of its curcumin extract in supplement form can be risky

items.title: A golden age of maths is dawning and mathematicians are freaking out
items.summary: Mathematicians are stunned at the progress AI is making in solving advanced problems, leaving some questioning whether there will still be room for humans

items.title: How human error became a weapon against large language models
items.summary: Alan Turing proposed a test for machine intelligence: could a computer convince a human it was human? We have begun conducting the same test on ourselves, writes Max Moser

items.title: Huge study of Alzheimer’s genetics identifies new drug targets
items.summary: Almost 50 more genes have been flagged as being linked to Alzheimer’s, along with changes in activity in crucial cells that disappear as dementia progresses

items.title: Geoengineering can thicken Arctic sea ice, but for how long?
items.summary: Two companies are aiming to preserve Arctic ice by pumping water onto the sheet and letting it freeze, but only one of the trials found that this delayed melting in the summer
